registration_resources.title: Waitlisting Overview for Students
registration_resources.note: this is for students who want to register for a class that is at capacity/full; the waitlist goes away the Friday before classes start

registration_resources.title: Cancelling Registration
registration_resources.note: Students who plan to drop all of their registered courses prior to the first day of the term will need to initiate the Cancellation process.

registration_resources.title: Leave of Absence
registration_resources.note: Students who wish to drop all courses after the first day of the term will need to initiate the Student Leave of Absence process.

courses.name: Design and Evaluation of Human-Computer Interaction
courses.description: Human factors methods applied to interface requirements, design, prototyping, and evaluation. Concepts related to understanding user characteristics, design principles, usability analysis, methods and techniques for design and evaluation of the interface. The evaluation and design of the information presentation characteristics of a wide variety of interfaces: web sites (e-commerce), mobile applications, and information presentation systems (cockpits, instrumentation, etc.).

courses.name: Virtual Environments, Virtual Worlds, and Application
courses.description: A systematic introduction to the underpinnings of Virtual Environments (VE), Virtual Worlds, advanced displays and immersive technologies; and an overview of some of the applications areas particularly virtual engineering.
courses.prerequisites: A working knowledge of Unity programming is a prerequisite for this course. Students should acquire this skill prior to enrollment. If you do not have a foundational understanding of Unity programming, please do not enroll in this course.

courses.name: Computational Perception
courses.description: This class covers statistical and algorithmic methods for sensing, recognizing, and interpreting the activities of people by a computer. We will focus on machine perception techniques that facilitate and augment human-computer interaction. The main goal of the class is to introduce computational perception on both theoretical and practical levels. Participation in small groups to design, implement, and evaluate a prototype of a human-computer interaction system that uses one or more of the techniques covered in the lectures.
courses.prerequisites: This course is designed for students who possess advanced programming skills.

courses.name: Optimization Methods for Complex Designs
courses.description: Optimization involves finding the 'best' according to specified criteria. Review of a range of optimization methods from traditional nonlinear to modern evolutionary methods such as Genetic algorithms. Examination of how these methods can be used to solve a wide variety of design problems across disciplines, including mechanical systems design, biomedical device design, biomedical imaging, and interaction with digital medical data. Students will gain knowledge of numerical optimization algorithms and a sufficient understanding of the strengths and weaknesses of these algorithms to apply them appropriately in engineering design. Experience includes code writing and off-the-shelf routines. Numerous case studies of real-world situations in which problems were modeled and solved using advanced optimization techniques.
courses.prerequisites: Students should have taken Engr 160, Math 265, and have some computer programming experience.

courses.name: Computer Graphics and Geometric Modeling
courses.description: Fundamentals of computer graphics technology. Data structures. Parametric curve and surface modeling. Solid model representations. Applications in engineering design, analysis, and manufacturing.
courses.prerequisites: This course is for students with advanced programming skills. Programming experience in C, C++, basic knowledge in Linear Algebra.

courses.name: Perspectives in HCI
courses.description: An exploration of different perspectives in HCI that covers: inclusive and humane design; the intersection of technology and race, gender, age, and class; technology around the world; the impact of technology on democracy; social engineering and cybersecurity; and other relevant topics students would like to explore.
courses.delivery_mode: Online asynchronous course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.name: Organizational and Social Implications of Human Computer Interaction
courses.description: Examine opportunities and implications of information technologies and human computer interaction on social and organizational systems. Explore ethical and social issues appurtenant to human computer interaction, both from a proscriptive and prescriptive perspective. Develop informed perspective on human computer interaction. Implications on research and development programs.
courses.delivery_mode: Asynchronous course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.name: Design for Behavioral Change
courses.description: The exploration and design of educational experiences and artifacts as they relate to the social, emotional, and behavioral aspects of society.

courses.name: Design and Ethics
courses.description: Issues in ethics and decision-making as they relate to technology, design, design research, HCI, and the design industry.
courses.delivery_mode: Asynchronous course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.name: Scientific Methods in Human Computer Interaction
courses.description: Basics of hypothesis testing, experimental design, analysis and interpretation of data, and the ethical principles of human research as they apply to research in human computer interaction. Offered odd-numbered years.

courses.name: Qualitative Research Methods in HCI
courses.description: Introduction to qualitative research methods specific to human computer interaction and user experience research, both from an academic perspective and applied industry perspective. Conduct, write, and critique qualitative research. Examples and projects will be drawn from human computer interaction domains, e.g., mobile apps, web applications, automated vehicle, self-checkout stations, etc. Methods include focus groups, interviews, surveys, and many others.

courses.name: Foundations of Game-based Learning
courses.description: Theories, principles, and best practices of utilizing games in educational environments. Topics include the theoretical foundations of learning games and game play, identity development in online environments, and assessment of learning in and out of games.
courses.notes: The section is offered online, and attendance is required during the assigned meeting pattern. Please follow all deadlines set by the Instructor in Canvas.

courses.name: Advanced Learning Environments Design
courses.description: Exploration of advanced aspects of the learning environments design process. Application of analysis, design, development and production, evaluation, implementation, and project management principles. Theory and research in educational technology provides the foundation for design decisions. Focus on current trends in learning environment design and the production and use of educational technology.

courses.name: Computational Implementation and Prototyping in HCI
courses.description: Fundamental concepts of software programming and the practical use of the Python programming language. Assignments include user interaction and interface design, information visualization, as well as other computational HCI tools. Intended for graduate students without prior background in software development. Requires programming during class lectures.
courses.delivery_mode: Asynchronous course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.name: User Experience Methodologies
courses.description: Fundamentals of lean user experience design, discovery, and process.

courses.type: Research, Internship and General Elective Suggestions

courses.note: Tiffany will contact you if you have the option to enroll

courses.type: Research, Internship and General Elective Suggestions
courses.name: Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Art and Design
courses.description: Artificial intelligence tools for art and design: strategies, principles, and tools for AI in visual art, applied art, and creative industry practices. The aesthetics of AI systems in creative practices. An overview of working with AI as artistic and visual communication media including using AI and machine learning applications, histories of AI in the arts, generative deep learning systems, supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks, and integrating AI systems through art and design practices. Producing AI multimedia outputs that include a range of forms from imagery to sound.

courses.type: Research, Internship and General Elective Suggestions
courses.name: Design Thinking
courses.description: Exploration of design thinking process, toolkits, and mindsets as creative problem solving approaches for systems, products, and processes, across diverse contexts. Strategies for problem-framing, creative solutions and co-evolution process, with a focus on collaborative and interdisciplinary design to investigate real-world problems and opportunities.
courses.delivery_mode: Asynchronous online course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.type: Research, Internship and General Elective Suggestions
courses.name: Legal and Ethical Issues in Cyber Security
courses.description: Legal and ethical issues in computer security. State and local codes and regulations. Privacy issues.
courses.delivery_mode: Asynchronous course; there are no scheduled lectures or meeting times.

courses.type: Research, Internship and General Elective Suggestions
courses.name: Core Studies: Professional Communication
courses.description: Covers various topics in AI. Contact the instructor, Abram Anders, if you have any questions.
